item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno if I already commented something on this, but this is one of my favorite SCP articles on the site. The absolute haste and urgency depicted in this article in such little time is outstanding. I especially love the incomplete sentence at the beginning that further drives that absolute urgency.

+1, a hundred times if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dental horror is extremely disturbing to me, for wisdom-tooth-related-near-death-experience-related reasons.

I initially disliked this because it seemed like a nasty picture with an uninspiring thing what kills and rapes you built around it, but then I paid closer attention and came to appreciate it. You've done a fantastic job of implying all sorts of horrible, disgusting toothiness without hemorrhaging blackboxes all over the [DATA EXPUNGED], which is difficult, impressive, and effective. I gotta say, using the phrase "transformative autocannibalism" without elaborating on it at all is one of the most frightening things you could've done. Good job.

Edit: I also appreciate the fact that you left the aforementioned horrible disgusting toothiness to the imagination, because that's far more horrifying and far less revulsion-inducing than actually describing what it does to people could ever be. I hate it when people spend a huge part of their SCP just describing all the various ways to mutilate somebody.

The rape does seem gratuitous, however.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that a recent edit was reverted as it changed the UK spelling variant of a word to the US spelling. UK spellings are considered stylistic choice, and should not be altered unless the author has given permission for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is honestly a perfect love song to the effective short-works Series I was defined by, and it's executed exceptionally well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is uninteresting and does not cause any emotions. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be honest, this object is awful. -1/100

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This both manages to do something that is simultaneously reminiscent of Series I SCPs and newly interesting to modern standards. This really could have gone the route of just being a generic, murderous monster, but coupled with the tone evoked by the short, potent description and uniquely intriguing anomaly here, this makes for a really enjoyable read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A perfect example of why I wish I could give half votes. You clearly set out to give an idea of S1, which you accomplished beautifully while including the most viscerally disgusting evocative language I've seen in awhile. Dental Violence was an absolute displeasure to read.

But the article really doesn't give me enough to work with. I don't get a real sense of a story, and while some are fine with that, inferred narratives or narrative seeds never capture my interest. Unfortunately, I had to round down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the concept seems interesting in theory, the article just doesn't have enough substance for me to enjoy it and ends up looking incredibly sloppy.

I get that it has to give readers some Series I vibes, which it did deliver, but at the same time, it reminds me of the type of Series I objects that I was never a big fan of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well the fact that it's called a "predatory" implies it's similar to an Earth animal and just attacking for food/because it's angry/territorial/that sort of thing, as for the people it doesn't kill it's apparently raping them and successfully reproducing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Appreciate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Job's done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am not saying this is literally literally a copy of an object. 

This is literally SCP-478 in a rather shoddy and lazy disguise. -1

Then don't say it literally is. If it's too similar, that's one thing, but that comment is a step away from accusing me of outright plagiarism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Except 478 provokes dental growth in the stomach, lungs, under the skin, so on. In short, they produce said teeth everywhere, quite like this one. Except without the explosive traits that quite honestly make it sound to me like an attempt to outwit. [insert witticizm about properly reading an article before retorting]

So, a) close enough; b) as it is apparently neccessary to elaborate, I am not saying this is literally literally a copy of an object. I am saying that it is a rather shallow attempt to tread a topic that has been already put into article form much, much better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Except 478 are fairies that hide in one's face and cause overproduction of teeth in the mouth/esophagus, and this is a tooth-based something that causes full-body teeth production, is infohazardous, and has horrible eldritch sex with anything it percieves as a threat.

The only thing they have in common is 'lots of teeth happen', but even how that functions is wildly different.

So, no.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is basically a rather shoddy and lazy rehash of a topic SCP-478 and other dental objects have already touched upon quite well. -1

[Edited under notable recommendation for the sake of clarity.]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funny enough, I love this a lot for all the idiosyncrasies complained about upthread. Is "dental violence" a ludicrously abstruse term in a supposedly clinical document? Well, yes. Is it also an absolute delicacy of a phrase? Hell yes. Maybe I'm in the minority here but I'd be super pumped to see a whole wave of Series V articles in this style if the language continues to be this evocative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good catch. Changed the wording to "intercourse".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-4910 seems to be capable of differentiating between those unaware of its properties—typically civilians—whom it uses for sustenance; and those who specifically seek to either capture or harm it—such as personnel of both mundane and occult organisations—whom it targets for the purposes of sexual reproduction.

While similar in most respects to standard victims, those targeted for reproduction act as a vector for SCP-4910's anomalous properties.

If the second paragraph here is the entirety of what happens to those targeted for reproduction, then it doesn't seem like sexual reproduction to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can see where people are coming from when they say that, but I think it's quite a compliment for people to be reminded of Moto42's writing just from the article alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While interesting in concept, there's just not enough on here to get me hooked. It's a monster that kills you for no real reason. No backstory, no nothing.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've put this better than I ever could. I was floundering between novote and upvote, and ultimately decided that at the end of the day:

  1. I have no complaints that aren't stylistic.
  2. This article weaponizes terseness in such a manner that adding much more would detract from its charms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh I get it now. Yeah this is great.
But now I can't do that slime girl SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The document mentions that those with knowledge of it's properties become targeted for reproduction, propagating it's effect further. Therefore, either it's vague to prevent this, or they simply don't know that much because of this aspect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah that's cool, my problem isn't a lack of specificity, but rather that without elaboration of any kind "dental quadraped" is meaningless. Compare to "dental bridge" and "dental dam". These aren't large raparine structures made of teeth!

Although if they have its tracks they actually can say a lot about its size, mass, and shape.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could take it a step further and add that they only know it's four-legged by observing its tracks…

But it can't be said for certain that it's anything-sized, or that it's shaped in a way reminiscient of any one animal, or anything of the sort.

Someone might depict it the way you describe, another might portray it as a single, large shuffling tooth, a third may depict it as a wyrm covered entirely in smiles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's definitely an improvement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tried adding this:

Artistic representation varies wildly, yet focuses exclusively on various algamations of teeth and the associated gingiva.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How do they know what they know, that helps. If it's eyewitnesses, then something like "Described as dog-sized quadraped; carapace composed of dental enamel." Fits the clipped style here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, I'm going to have to go ahead and backtrack and better explain.
It's not 100% composed of just teeth - that much is clear from the image. This is pretty much what I'm getting at:

means it somehow have something to do with teeth, and it's not immediately clear if it's its composition, its habitat, its shape, or what.

…because it's not clear or known. The only information they have to go on is that it is somehow relating to teeth.

"Predatory quadruped that is in some way, shape, or form either made of teeth, appears like teeth, has something to do with teeth, or is somehow relating to teeth in some manner" doesn't quite have the same ring to it.

If there's a superior way to phrase that first bit, I'm open to suggestion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So wait I'm confused, is there an entity effecting the document that makes people document it vaguely?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dental doesn't really mean made of teeth tho. It's just means "of teeth"—like, dental replacements aren't made of actual teeth because that's the point: you don't replace teeth with teeth. Dental hygiene, dental health, dental specialist — I haven't really seen any usage of "dental" to mean "made of teeth".

Dental quadrupeds would only means it somehow have something to do with teeth, and it's not immediately clear if it's its composition, its habitat, its shape, or what.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If they know it's made out of teeth, they can say that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is exactly a case of not having the data.

Precise details unknown

All that is known regarding its physiology is that it's four legged and made out of teeth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The writing style feel like notes than an informative document made to explain all of its qualities. It does that but feels like a newbie's coldpost. I like how short it is but the lack of details about stuff like a "dental quadruped" is bothersome. That doesn't tell me much about how it looks. It is a bit of a confusing read, and a bit of a slog just to decipher what is going on exactly.

Despite what may be a really good concept, I must downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's also where I downvoted. The article is short, I'm fine with that, but it's cutting too much descriptors about what we know. It's not the case of "we don't have data on this" but "we have data on this but we'll not tell you even if we have no reason to withhold it"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see what you're saying, but I actually like this style, and think it works well for the tone the article's going for. Point taken, though — in retrospect I should probably have said "any major narrative element to add".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's a "dental quadraped" though? Does it have teeth for legs? This is where I downvoted. It really feels like a useless descriptor, to me. Maybe I am missing something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well, I 'm not feeling the accidental channeling of Moto42 here.. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't think of anything to add that would make it better.

Complete sentences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
None of the successful short articles the community has produced are anything like game demos, unless you can somehow demonstrate where they're being used to give a demonstration of a larger work for someone to purchase. They're no more "demos" for long-form articles than a minimalist painting is a "demo" for an exhaustively-detailed fresco. They're complete works.

If anything, I can agree that this lacks a traditional "narrative" — even by SCP article standard — but formulaic construction and closure isn't the intent here: This is a tableau.

I am interested in how you think short works are going to actually harm the site. It's not an opinion I've seen anyone express anywhere else, so I am curious as to whatever unique insight you possess that would cause you to make such a bold claim.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe I'm just a 15 y/o boomer for Series 3 articles 3ven though I was never apart of it. S3 did have short articles, but they were miles better than the demos that S4/V articles are and were more like short games like Doom 2 or something. What made S3 good to me was how it was heavily sci-fi themed and very futuristic so to speak.

Maybe if I return to writing I'll write like that. Maybe the community will shift to something else by then. Who knows?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The community will upvote what it likes. Currently, people seem to be jonesing for short, interesting, Series-I-style articles; therefore, it's up to authors to provide them that. We'll always have long articles, and we'll always have short articles, and it's good to have a mix of both that prevents the site from becoming stuck in a role.

Regarding this one… I can't think of anything to add that would make it better. It's scary on a fundamental level, partly because of the brevity; it uses the reader's own imaginations to more effectively induce emotion. That's not "unfinished concept", that's "calculated work of fiction".

That said, maybe I'm just not good enough at expanding works. If you've got a suggestion, I'd love to hear it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They're like demos of games, but are released and marketed as the final product. Many short articles are demos of what could be an amazing article but feel like they got cut off abruptly such as this one. I'm not saying that everything has to be war and peace in length because there are a few long articles I hate.

What I do have a problem with is the popularization of these demo articles over longer articles as I feel that it will do more harm in the long run

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's your problem with short articles?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was certainly ill advised to go off on a tangent about the general constraits of consistency, which is a wider issue that crops up all over the place; and a decent-sized dick move to mock them.

That being said, this is only getting more and more off-topic. Back to SCP discussion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not all of those hundreds of MTFs feature equally in existing written works. We have an extended list here: http://www.scp-wiki.net/task-forces-complete-list

ETA: okay, read through the rest of the conversation and saw how complicated it got.

The gist of it is that the primary MTF list is for the MTFs that fulfill a certain criteria/number of related works and authors. The extended list is for all MTFs and where they're mentioned, regardless of how many associated pages or authors they have.

Authors are not limited to only using the content in either list. Authors making up their own content is how new entries are added to those lists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is extremely series 1, and I love it. I hate that picture with every fiber of my being though.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was a newbie not knowing some site knowledge

There was no need to go sicko mode on him

He wasn't clueless, he wasn't derailing a convo, he simply didn't know a piece of site knowledge/culture/whatever which is more common than rain in April for newbs and using fancy words isn't gonna help them out any time soon

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm well within my right to defend my choices, which were carefully explained by myself and others, against repeated, patently clueless observations and misunderstandings that are doing nothing but derailing discussion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: pretty sure this is just a list of MTFs that appear in multiple articles

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, there are literally hundreds of task forces. Why does this exist then? http://www.scp-wiki.net/task-forces

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed. The pacing and relative large size of the containment procedures juxtaposed with the image make for engaging reading.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really fond of the writing style here. Less is more, sometimes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lore on this site is a joke anyways

We got more retcons than Metal Gear because of the pages that attempt to explain it and the 20 or so articles linked on said page that can contradict it.

Yes we do have cannons and so on, but it is fine there because they blatantly say "hey we are breaking the traditional "lore" here"

And it isn't good to be defending it by being confrontational and smug

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just because it's in an article doesn't make it part of the lore.

HAHAHAHAHAHA

Oh. You're serious?

Lore is created as successful works are created. One in the same. This is part of the lore, as others have already helpfully lined out.

I'll have to bring this out a step further though: This kind of "Thou shalt not" stance is the type of bullshit that inhibits creativity, experimentation, and expression.

If someone has a different idea for how things operate1, so long as it is internally consistent, more power to them. All that matters is if the story itself is entertaining. We don't grow or evolve by writing samey shit en masse.

Perfectly acceptable reason to downvote, if indeed it's truly that abhorrent to name something slightly different, but it just looks asinine to maintain that everything should neatly fall in line with everything else that exists on the mainlist.

Stamping one's feet and pointing to a site resource as if it's the end-all be-all of what someone is allowed to do is foolish.

Footnotes
1. Granted, this is an incredibly minor difference - I'm of the opinion that we've not nearly enough works that display a vastly different Foundation, say, such as that in Broken Masquerade, unconcerned with maintaining the veil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a neat 001 proposal idea right there, a commandment from God for humanity to protect themselves

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Where on the Mobile Task Force page does it say that 'MTF Epsilon doesn't exist'?

If you think that task forces that don't appear on that page don't exist, there are like a thousand other random task forces.

also, the lore is literally just stuff that appears in articles

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would rather opt for good an engaging writing rather than stick to an arbitrary set of rules. The format is a tool you use to craft a piece of writing. You can cherry-pick the parts you do and do not want really, so long as the end product is engaging.

This is an example of good writing over rule following.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lore? Is every article supposed to be part of the lore?
